claims.claim_text: The opening 03:05 regional outage anecdote presents a specific incident without an attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: At 03:05, a platform engineer watches the deployment board turn red across one region. The application code was already tested, the image existed in the registry, and the Git change looked small: one version bump and one environment variable addition. The cluster did exactly what it had been told to do, but nobody had modeled what should happen when a required cluster substitution was missing, a dependency was only applied rather than healthy, and the rollout notification path only reported success.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform team can still trigger a failed rollout after a seemingly small Git change when required substitutions, readiness dependencies, and notification paths were not modeled correctly.
claims.rationale: This is a named, time-specific incident narrative with precise operational details but no cited real-world source.

claims.claim_text: Flux is built as the GitOps Toolkit: a set of specialized Kubernetes controllers and composable APIs rather than a single deployment application.
claims.span_hint: section: Read Flux as a Reconciliation System, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a product-architecture claim about how Flux is structured and should point to upstream component documentation.

claims.claim_text: Flux source-controller supports source objects such as GitRepository, HelmRepository, OCIRepository, and Bucket to fetch artifacts for other controllers.
claims.span_hint: section: Read Flux as a Reconciliation System, controller overview and table
claims.rationale: This is a concrete upstream capability claim about supported Flux source types and source-controller behavior.

claims.claim_text: Flux can be installed with only selected controllers, and the minimum required bootstrap components are source-controller and kustomize-controller.
claims.span_hint: section: Read Flux as a Reconciliation System, paragraph beginning 'That separation gives Flux a composable shape.'
claims.rationale: The module makes a specific installation-scope claim that upstream installation docs directly describe.

claims.claim_text: Flux bootstrap for GitHub installs controllers, commits Flux manifests into the chosen repository path, and configures Flux to manage itself from Git.
claims.span_hint: section: Bootstrap Sources Before You Reconcile Workloads, first three paragraphs
claims.rationale: This is a specific behavior of `flux bootstrap github` and should be backed by the official bootstrap guide.

claims.claim_text: Flux Kustomization supports pruning, readiness waiting, health checks, dependency ordering with `dependsOn`, and post-build substitution controls.
claims.span_hint: section: Apply Workloads Safely with Kustomization and HelmRelease
claims.rationale: These are detailed product features of the Kustomization API and need an upstream reference.

claims.claim_text: HelmRelease is the Flux resource that manages Helm chart lifecycle from HelmRepository or GitRepository sources.
claims.span_hint: section: Apply Workloads Safely with Kustomization and HelmRelease, HelmRelease introduction and examples
claims.rationale: This is a direct Helm controller capability claim about what HelmRelease does and which source types it consumes.

claims.claim_text: Flux ImagePolicy can select image tags using semver, alphabetical, or numerical policies.
claims.span_hint: section: Automate Images Without Bypassing Git, ImagePolicy discussion and controller table
claims.rationale: The tag-selection modes are concrete documented capabilities and should point to the ImagePolicy docs.

claims.claim_text: Flux image automation uses ImageRepository, ImagePolicy, and ImageUpdateAutomation, and updates Git by editing marked fields with setters.
claims.span_hint: section: Automate Images Without Bypassing Git, automation flow and setter-marker explanation
claims.rationale: This is a specific workflow claim about how Flux image automation mutates Git-managed manifests.

claims.claim_text: Flux notification providers support destinations such as Slack and Microsoft Teams, and can update GitHub commit status.
claims.span_hint: section: Automate Images Without Bypassing Git, Notifications subsection
claims.rationale: These are concrete notification-controller provider capabilities that should cite the provider documentation.

claims.claim_text: Flux v2 was a complete rewrite from the earlier monolithic Flux v1 into the current specialized-controller toolkit model.
claims.rationale: This is a specific historical architecture claim explicitly covered by the Flux migration FAQ.

claims.claim_text: Flux documents vertical scaling through controller resource tuning and increased concurrent reconciliations.
claims.span_hint: section: Operate Flux Across Clusters and Incidents, paragraph beginning 'Scaling Flux can mean increasing controller resources'
claims.rationale: This is a concrete operational capability claim about official scaling knobs.

claims.claim_text: Flux supports controller sharding for horizontal scaling by assigning labeled resources to different controller instances.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3; section: Operate Flux Across Clusters and Incidents, scaling paragraph
claims.rationale: The module names a specific scaling mechanism that is directly documented by Flux.

claims.claim_text: Weaveworks coined the term GitOps in 2017.
claims.rationale: This is a dated historical claim and the cited CNCF article states it explicitly.

claims.claim_text: Flux is a CNCF graduated project.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4; comparison paragraph stating Flux and ArgoCD are mature CNCF projects
claims.rationale: Project maturity status is a named governance fact and should cite CNCF directly.

claims.claim_text: Argo is a CNCF graduated project.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4; comparison paragraph stating Flux and ArgoCD are mature CNCF projects
claims.rationale: The Argo project page directly states its CNCF maturity level.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD exposes a central API server consumed by its Web UI, CLI, and CI/CD systems.
claims.span_hint: section: Read Flux as a Reconciliation System, comparison paragraph beginning 'When comparing Flux to ArgoCD'
claims.rationale: This is a specific Argo CD architecture claim used in the Flux-vs-Argo comparison.

claims.claim_text: Flux repository-structure guidance uses dedicated cluster directories and separates infrastructure from applications so each cluster state is defined in its own path and ordering remains reviewable.
claims.span_hint: section: Bootstrap Sources Before You Reconcile Workloads, repository layout discussion; section: Operate Flux Across Clusters and Incidents, multi-cluster opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: The module makes a concrete claim about recommended Flux repository organization that the official guide documents.

further_reading.title: Flux Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Good companion reading for the reconciliation model, source types, and controller roles introduced in this module.

further_reading.title: Flux Kustomization API
further_reading.why_relevant: Best follow-up reference for `dependsOn`, `wait`, health checks, pruning, and substitution behavior.

further_reading.title: Automate Image Updates to Git
further_reading.why_relevant: Expands the module’s image-automation section with end-to-end setup details and policy examples.

notes: Ignored quiz items, hands-on steps, diagrams/code blocks, and most connective teaching prose per the seed rules. `source_ids` are left empty because no shared citation pool was assumed for this run; each supported claim carries a proposed upstream URL instead.
